sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=55, col=30).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 95, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 94 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 2.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image:
1. The grid now has all gray cells (no green cells)
2. The bar cursor moved from the right end to a position closer to the middle
3. The left panel still has purple cells in a cross pattern
So clicking on the center cell changed all grid cells from green/gray to all gray. This is interesting.
Let me think about what happened:
- Before: cross cells were green, corner cells were gray
- After: all cells are gray
So clicking on the center cell "reset" the grid to all gray.
But wait, the bar cursor also moved. It was at the right end, and now it's closer to the middle. This suggests that the bar cursor position is related to the grid state.
Let me think about this more carefully. The bar has:
- Left: blue
- Middle: gray
- Right: blue
The cursor is a vertical blue line. When the cursor is at the right end, clicking on cells changes their color. When the cursor is at the left end, clicking on cells doesn't change their color (but the cursor moves to the right end).
Now the cursor is in the middle of the bar. Maybe the cursor position represents the "progress" of the level. When the cursor is at the right end, the level is not complete. When the cursor is at the left end, the level is complete.
But the level is not completed yet (level_completed: false). So maybe the cursor needs to reach the left end to complete the level.
Actually, looking at the bar more carefully, the cursor seems to be moving towards the left end. Initially, it was at the right end. After clicking on cells, it moved towards the left end.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the bar represents the "remaining work" to complete the level. When the cursor is at the right end, there's a lot of work to do. As I click on cells, the cursor moves towards the left end, indicating progress. When the cursor reaches the left end, the level is complete.
But clicking on the center cell reset the grid to all gray, and the cursor moved towards the left end. This suggests that clicking on the center cell made progress towards completing the level.
Let me try clicking on the center cell again to see if the cursor moves further to the left.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the bar. The bar has:
- Left: blue square (cursor start position?)
- Middle: gray bar
- Right: blue square (cursor end position?)
The cursor is a vertical blue line on the gray bar. When the cursor is at the right end, it's near the right blue square. When the cursor is at the left end, it's near the left blue square.
Looking at the current image, the cursor is in the middle of the gray bar, closer to the right end than the left end. So the cursor has moved from the right end towards the left end.
Let me try clicking on the center cell again to see if the cursor moves further to the left.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the current grid state and bar cursor position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Grid cells:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    boundary = node['boundary']
    min_row = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
    max_row = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
    min_col = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
    max_col = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
    if 49 <= min_row <= 61 and 24 <= min_col <= 36:
        center_row = (min_row + max_row) // 2
        center_col = (min_col + max_col) // 2
        print(f"  center=({center_row}, {center_col}), color={node['color']}")

# Check bar cursor position
print("\nBar nodes:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    boundary = node['boundary']
    min_row = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
    max_row = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
    min_col = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
    max_col = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
    if 10 <= min_row <= 20 and 15 <= min_col <= 40:
        center_row = (min_row + max_row) // 2
        center_col = (min_col + max_col) // 2
        print(f"  center=({center_row}, {center_col}), color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}")

# Try clicking on the center cell again
print("\nClicking on center cell...")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 30}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
